message:
feat(ftui): add canonical keybinding table and input dispatcher (FTUI-06.1)

Create keymap.rs as the single source of truth for all wa TUI keybindings:
- Declarative KEYMAP table: 60+ bindings across 6 scopes (Global, Panes,
  Events, Triage, History, Search)
- Deterministic resolve() dispatcher: global bindings always win over
  view-specific, with fallback heuristics for text input
- Action enum covering all TUI intents (navigation, filtering, triage,
  search, profile management)
- KeyPattern matching against framework-agnostic KeyInput/Key types
- action_label() and bindings_for_scope() for help text generation
- 25 parity tests verifying all views match legacy behavior

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-1wg","title":"[EPIC] Backup & Restore: Database Export, Import, Scheduled Backups","description":"# [EPIC] Backup & Restore System

## Mission
Provide users with confidence that their terminal history and configuration will never be lost, and make migration between machines or versions painless.

## Why This Matters
wa accumulates valuable data:
- Terminal transcripts (searchable history)
- Event timeline (debugging context)
- Configuration (tuned patterns, workflows)

Loss of this data would be a significant trust violation. Users need:
- Peace of mind that data is safe
- Easy migration to new machines
- Recovery from corruption or mistakes

## Components

### 1. Database Export (`wa backup export`)
Export the entire database to a portable format:
```bash
$ wa backup export --output backup_2026-01-18.wa
Creating backup...
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Patterns: 23
  Config: included
Backup saved: backup_2026-01-18.wa (45 MB)
SHA256: abc123...
```

Export format:
- Self-describing archive (version header + manifest)
- SQLite dump + JSON metadata
- Optional compression (zstd)
- Integrity checksum

### 2. Database Import (`wa backup import`)
Restore from a backup:
```bash
$ wa backup import backup_2026-01-18.wa --verify
Verifying backup integrity... OK
Backup version: 1.2.0 (compatible)
Contents:
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Config: included

Restore options:
  [1] Full restore (replace current data)
  [2] Merge (keep newer, import older)
  [3] Dry-run (show what would change)
```

Safety:
- Require explicit confirmation for full restore
- Create automatic backup before import
- Validate schema compatibility

### 3. Incremental Backups
For users who want continuous protection:
```toml
[backup]
enabled = true
schedule = "daily"  # or "hourly", "weekly"
retention_days = 30
destination = "~/.local/share/wa/backups/"
max_backups = 10
```

### 4. Configuration Export/Import
Separate from data, for sharing configs:
```bash
$ wa config export --output my-config.toml
$ wa config import my-config.toml --merge
```

### 5. Migration Assistant
When upgrading wa with breaking changes:
```bash
$ wa upgrade --from 1.x
Detected wa 1.x database
Migration required for:
  - Schema v1 → v2 (new event columns)
  - Config format update

Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Success Criteria
A user should be able to:
- create a backup file that is self-contained and verifiable (manifest + checksums)
- restore safely with a preview/dry-run path before any destructive change
- migrate between machines without manual file spelunking

Operational expectations (v0):
- backups are deterministic and explainable (stable manifest, clear versions)
- backups do not leak secrets (config redaction + no raw transcript dumping)
- imports refuse unsafe/incompatible inputs with actionable errors
- scheduled backups are robust (bounded disk usage, retention policy enforced)

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Export/import round-trip preserves all data
  - Version detection works across schema versions
  - Merge logic handles conflicts correctly

- Integration tests:
  - Backup with active watcher (snapshot isolation)
  - Import into empty vs populated database
  - Scheduled backup runs correctly

- E2E tests:
  - Full backup/restore cycle with real data
  - Migration from mock old version
  - Corrupt backup rejected with clear error

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] wa backup export creates portable backup files
- [ ] wa backup import restores with verification
- [ ] Incremental backup scheduling works
- [ ] wa config export/import for config-only migration
- [ ] Version migration assistant for upgrades
- [ ] All backups include integrity checksums
- [ ] Clear error messages for incompatible backups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:55:22.915697374Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963666064Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T02:58:36.963516365Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-reliability","area-ux","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-1wg","depends_on_id":"wa-37x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:24Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-2ep","title":"[EPIC] Deep Explainability: wa why + Enriched Errors + Decision Tracing","description":"# [EPIC] Deep Explainability System

## Mission
Make every wa decision **transparent and understandable**. When wa allows, denies, or takes any action, users should be able to understand exactly why.

## Why This Is Critical
Terminal automation is scary because it is opaque. The #1 question users ask about any automation is: **"Why did it do that?"**

Without explainability:
- Users distrust the tool
- Debugging requires diving into logs
- Configuration is trial-and-error
- Support burden is high

With explainability:
- Users understand and trust decisions
- Debugging is self-service
- Configuration is informed
- The tool teaches as it operates

## Components

### 1. `wa why` Command
A dedicated command to explain any recent decision:
```bash
$ wa why denied --pane 3
Decision: DENY
Reason: Pane 3 is in AltScreen mode
Evidence:
  - AltScreen flag detected at 2026-01-18T14:32:01
  - Policy rule: safety.alt_screen_block (severity: hard_deny)
  - Rationale: Sending input while AltScreen is active can corrupt TUI applications

To proceed:
  1. Wait for pane to exit AltScreen mode, OR
  2. Use the approval flow:
     - run: wa approve <allow_once_code>
     - retry the original command
```

### 2. Enriched Error Messages
Every error includes actionable remediation, preferring **safe** commands over “delete files” guidance.

Example:
```bash
$ wa watch
Error: Cannot start watcher - workspace lock is held

To fix:
  - See who is running it: wa doctor
  - Stop the watcher safely: wa stop
  - If the lock appears stale, follow wa doctor’s instructions (do not guess)

Then retry: wa watch
```

```bash
$ wa send --pane 99 "hello"
Error: Pane 99 not found

Available panes: 1, 3, 7, 9
Did you mean:   wa send --pane 9 "hello"  (closest match)
List all:       wa status
```

### 3. Decision Context Capture
PolicyEngine and workflow runner capture full decision context (not just result):
- Input conditions (capabilities, state)
- Rules evaluated
- Why each rule matched or did not match
- Final decision with confidence

### 4. Explanation Templates
Human-readable explanations for every rule and decision type:
- Map `(rule_id, decision)` → explanation + remediation
- Keep templates in sync with rules (enforce via tests)

## Design Principles
1. **No jargon**: Explanations use plain English
2. **Actionable**: Every explanation includes "To fix:" steps
3. **Contextual**: Show evidence from the actual situation
4. **Progressive**: Brief summary first, details on request

## Testing
- Unit tests: Every rule has an explanation template
- Integration tests: `wa why` returns expected explanations for known scenarios
- UX tests: Explanations are understandable by non-experts (user testing)

## Success Criteria
- `wa why <decision-id>` (or equivalent) explains any recent policy decision/workflow action
- All error messages include "To fix:" with specific steps
- Explanations point to safe next actions (e.g., `wa stop`, `wa doctor`, `wa approve`) and avoid unsafe “just delete X” advice
- Users can self-diagnose the majority of issues without external help

## Acceptance Criteria
- wa why returns a structured decision trace with redacted context.
- Enriched errors include cause chain and remediation hints.
- Trace output is stable and machine-parseable.

 {"id":"wa-2ji","title":"Stress test: scalability with 50+ panes and large transcripts","description":"# Task: Scalability Stress Tests

## Goal
Validate wa performance and stability under high load conditions.

## Test Scenarios

### 1. Many Panes (50+)
```bash
# Setup: Create 50 panes with concurrent activity
for i in {1..50}; do
    wezterm cli spawn --new-window -- bash -c "while true; do echo line $i; sleep 0.1; done" &
done

# Run watcher and measure:
wa watch &
sleep 60

# Metrics to collect:
# - CPU usage (should stay < 50%)
# - Memory usage (should stay < 500MB)
# - Ingest lag (should stay < 100ms)
# - Queue depth (should not grow unbounded)
```

### 2. Large Transcript (1M+ lines)
```bash
# Generate large output in single pane
for i in {1..1000000}; do
    echo "Log line $i with some content to match patterns against"
done

# Verify:
# - FTS indexing completes
# - Search remains responsive (< 500ms for broad query)
# - Memory usage bounded
# - No disk space explosion
```

### 3. High Event Rate
```bash
# Trigger many pattern matches rapidly
for i in {1..1000}; do
    echo "ERROR: test error $i"
    sleep 0.01
done

# Verify:
# - Event deduplication works
# - No event queue overflow
# - Notifications throttled appropriately
```

### 4. Long-Running Stability (24h)
```bash
# Run watcher for extended period with normal activity
wa watch &
# ... normal usage for 24 hours ...

# Check for:
# - Memory leaks (memory growth over time)
# - File handle leaks
# - Database size growth rate
# - No crashes or hangs
```

## Metrics Collection
```rust
struct StressTestMetrics {
    cpu_usage_percent: Vec<f32>,
    memory_mb: Vec<f32>,
    ingest_lag_ms: Vec<f32>,
    queue_depth: Vec<usize>,
    events_per_second: f32,
    fts_query_p99_ms: f32,
}
```

## Pass Criteria
| Scenario | Metric | Budget |
|----------|--------|--------|
| 50 panes | CPU idle | < 50% |
| 50 panes | Memory | < 500MB |
| 50 panes | Ingest lag | < 100ms |
| 1M lines | FTS query | < 500ms |
| 1M lines | Memory | < 1GB |
| 24h run | Memory growth | < 10% |
| 24h run | Crashes | 0 |

## Testing
- CI integration: run scaled-down version (10 panes, 100k lines)
- Manual: full stress test before releases
- Artifacts: metrics graphs, memory profiles

## Acceptance Criteria
- Stress test suite exists and is runnable
- Budget thresholds are enforced
- Regressions are visible in CI
- Long-running test detects leaks

 {"id":"wa-4df","title":"[Human command] `wa backup export` (database + config archive with checksum)","description":"# Task: wa backup export

## Goal
Create portable backup archives that users can store, copy, and restore from.

## Command Design
```bash
# Basic export
$ wa backup export
Backup saved: ~/.local/share/wa/backups/wa_backup_2026-01-18_143015.wa

# Custom output
$ wa backup export --output /path/to/backup.wa

# With compression
$ wa backup export --compress

# Include specific workspaces only
$ wa backup export --workspace /project/a --workspace /project/b

# Exclude large transcript data (config/events only)
$ wa backup export --metadata-only
```

## Output Format
Archive structure (tar + optional zstd):
```
backup.wa/
├── manifest.json      # Version, timestamp, checksums
├── schema_version     # Plain text: "3"
├── database.sql       # SQLite dump
├── config.toml        # Effective configuration
├── patterns/          # Custom pattern packs
│   └── custom.toml
└── checksums.sha256   # Per-file checksums
```

manifest.json:
```json
{
  "wa_version": "0.1.0",
  "schema_version": 3,
  "created_at": "2026-01-18T14:30:15Z",
  "workspaces": ["/project/a", "/project/b"],
  "stats": {
    "segments": 12345,
    "events": 567,
    "panes": 8
  },
  "checksum": "sha256:abc123..."
}
```

## Implementation Notes
- Use SQLite `.dump` for portability
- Acquire read lock during export (snapshot isolation)
- Stream large exports to avoid memory issues
- Verify checksum immediately after write

## Testing
- Unit tests: archive format validation, manifest generation
- Integration: export with active watcher, verify no corruption
- E2E: export + import round-trip preserves all data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Export creates valid, portable archive
- Checksum included and verified on write
- Progress shown for large databases
- Metadata-only mode for quick config backup

 {"id":"wa-4r7","title":"Multi-account exhaustion handling: all accounts at limit → graceful degradation","description":"# Multi-account exhaustion handling

## Purpose
Handle the scenario where ALL configured accounts have reached their usage limits simultaneously.

## Problem
Current usage limit workflow assumes at least one account is available for failover.
When all accounts are exhausted, we need graceful degradation:
1. Don't attempt impossible failovers
2. Provide clear status to user
3. Schedule auto-resume at earliest reset time
4. Support notification of this critical state

## Detection
```rust
// In account selection logic
let available = accounts.iter()
    .filter(|a| a.percent_remaining > 0.0 || a.reset_time < now)
    .collect::<Vec<_>>();

if available.is_empty() {
    return Err(AccountExhausted {
        next_reset: accounts.iter()
            .map(|a| a.reset_time)
            .min(),
        accounts_checked: accounts.len(),
    });
}
```

## Workflow Behavior
When `AccountExhausted` detected:

### Step 1: Capture state
- Save current session transcript
- Record in-progress task (if determinable)
- Log which accounts were checked

### Step 2: Calculate resume time
```rust
let earliest_reset = accounts.iter()
    .filter_map(|a| a.reset_time)
    .min();
```

### Step 3: Persist "awaiting reset" state
```sql
INSERT INTO workflow_paused 
(pane_id, reason, resume_at, state_snapshot)
VALUES (?, 'all_accounts_exhausted', ?, ?);
```

### Step 4: Notify user
```
🔴 All accounts exhausted

All 3 configured OpenAI accounts have reached their usage limits.

Next reset: account-2 @ 2026-01-19 00:00 UTC (6h 23m)

Options:
  1. Wait for reset (wa will auto-resume if enabled)
  2. Add new account: wa accounts add
  3. Continue manually in exhausted account (with limits)

Status: wa accounts status
Resume: wa workflow resume --when-available
```

### Step 5: Schedule resume (optional)
If auto-resume enabled:
- Create timer/cron for earliest reset time
- Resume workflow when triggered

## Configuration
```toml
[accounts.exhaustion]
notify_channels = ["desktop", "webhook"]
auto_resume_on_reset = true
save_transcript = true
```

## Robot Mode Error
```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "error": {
    "code": "E_ALL_ACCOUNTS_EXHAUSTED",
    "message": "All configured accounts have reached usage limits",
    "data": {
      "accounts_checked": 3,
      "next_reset": "2026-01-19T00:00:00Z",
      "next_reset_account": "account-2"
    }
  }
}
```

## Testing
- Unit: Account selection returns exhaustion error correctly
- Unit: Resume time calculation
- Integration: Workflow pauses correctly
- E2E: Full exhaustion → notification → resume flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Exhaustion detected when all accounts at limit
- [ ] Clear user notification with reset times
- [ ] State preserved for resume
- [ ] Auto-resume works when enabled
- [ ] Robot mode returns structured error","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:14:14.002558283Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T17:30:34.037935162Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T17:30:34.037786926Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-accounts","area-workflows","phase-2"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"bd-35zb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"bd-u194","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-4r7","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.1.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:25Z","created_by":"import"}]}

+//! Canonical keybinding table and input dispatcher for wa TUI.
+//!
+//! All keybindings are defined declaratively in [`KEYMAP`] as
+//! `(KeyPattern, Scope, Action)` tuples.  The [`resolve`] function matches
+//! an incoming [`KeyInput`](super::ftui_compat::KeyInput) against the table
+//! for a given view, with global bindings checked first (deterministic
+//! conflict policy: **global wins over view-specific**).
+//!
+//! # Parity guarantee
+//!
+//! This module is the single source of truth for keybinding behavior.  Both
+//! the ratatui (`app.rs`) and ftui (`ftui_stub.rs`) backends should dispatch
+//! through [`resolve`] — or, during the migration period, maintain byte-level
+//! parity with the canonical table (verified by the `parity_` tests below).
+//!
+//! # Deletion criterion
+//! After the `tui` feature is dropped (FTUI-09.3), the legacy parity tests
+//! can be removed, but the keymap itself and the resolver remain.
+
+use super::ftui_compat::{Key, KeyInput};
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Scope
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Scope in which a keybinding is active.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
+pub enum Scope {
+    /// Active in every view (checked first).
+    Global,
+    /// Active only in the named view.
+    Panes,
+    Events,
+    Triage,
+    History,
+    Search,
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Action
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Canonical action produced by a keybinding.
+///
+/// Actions are framework-agnostic intents.  The event loop (legacy or ftui)
+/// translates them into the appropriate state mutation or command.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
+pub enum Action {
+    // -- global --
+    Quit,
+    ShowHelp,
+    Refresh,
+    NextTab,
+    PrevTab,
+    GoToView(u8), // 1-7
+
+    // -- list navigation (shared pattern across Panes/Events/Triage/History/Search) --
+    ListNext,
+    ListPrev,
+
+    // -- filter / text input --
+    FilterAppendChar(char),
+    FilterDeleteChar,
+    FilterClear,
+
+    // -- panes --
+    ToggleUnhandledOnly,
+    ToggleBookmarkedOnly,
+    CycleAgentFilter,
+    CycleDomainFilter,
+    CycleRulesetProfile,
+    ApplyRulesetProfile,
+
+    // -- events --
+    EventsFilterDigit(char),
+
+    // -- triage --
+    TriagePrimaryAction,
+    TriageMute,
+    TriageToggleExpand,
+    TriageNumberedAction(u8), // 1-9
+
+    // -- history --
+    ToggleUndoableOnly,
+
+    // -- search --
+    SearchNextSaved,
+    SearchPrevSaved,
+    SearchRunSaved,
+    SearchToggleSaved,
+    SearchExecute,
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// KeyPattern — declarative key matcher
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// A pattern that matches a key input.
+///
+/// Created via helper functions below for readability.
+#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
+pub struct KeyPattern {
+    pub key: Key,
+    pub ctrl: bool,
+    pub alt: bool,
+    pub shift: bool,
+}
+
+impl KeyPattern {
+    const fn matches(&self, input: &KeyInput) -> bool {
+        if self.ctrl != input.ctrl || self.alt != input.alt || self.shift != input.shift {
+            return false;
+        }
+        match (&self.key, &input.key) {
+            (Key::Char(a), Key::Char(b)) => *a == *b,
+            (Key::Enter, Key::Enter)
+            | (Key::Esc, Key::Esc)
+            | (Key::Tab, Key::Tab)
+            | (Key::BackTab, Key::BackTab)
+            | (Key::Backspace, Key::Backspace)
+            | (Key::Up, Key::Up)
+            | (Key::Down, Key::Down)
+            | (Key::Left, Key::Left)
+            | (Key::Right, Key::Right)
+            | (Key::Home, Key::Home)
+            | (Key::End, Key::End)
+            | (Key::PageUp, Key::PageUp)
+            | (Key::PageDown, Key::PageDown)
+            | (Key::Delete, Key::Delete) => true,
+            (Key::F(a), Key::F(b)) => *a == *b,
+            _ => false,
+        }
+    }
+}
+
+/// Plain key, no modifiers.
+const fn key(k: Key) -> KeyPattern {
+    KeyPattern {
+        key: k,
+        ctrl: false,
+        alt: false,
+        shift: false,
+    }
+}
+
+/// Ctrl + key.
+const fn ctrl(k: Key) -> KeyPattern {
+    KeyPattern {
+        key: k,
+        ctrl: true,
+        alt: false,
+        shift: false,
+    }
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Binding entry
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// A single entry in the canonical keymap.
+struct Binding {
+    pattern: KeyPattern,
+    scope: Scope,
+    action: Action,
+}
+
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// The canonical keymap
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// All wa TUI keybindings.  Order matters for resolution within a scope
+/// (first match wins), but in practice there should be no duplicates.
+static KEYMAP: &[Binding] = &[
+    // ---- Global ----
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('q')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::Quit },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('?')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::ShowHelp },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('r')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::Refresh },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Tab),        scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::NextTab },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::BackTab),    scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::PrevTab },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('1')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(1) },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('2')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(2) },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('3')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(3) },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('4')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(4) },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('5')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(5) },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('6')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(6) },
+    Binding { pattern: key(Key::Char('7')),  scope: Scope::Global, action: Action::GoToView(7) },
+
+    // ---- Panes ----

+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+// Resolver
+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+/// Convert a view name to its keymap scope.
+///
+/// Home and Help have no view-specific bindings, so they return `None`.
+fn view_scope(view_name: &str) -> Option<Scope> {
+    match view_name {
+        "Panes" => Some(Scope::Panes),
+        "Events" => Some(Scope::Events),
+        "Triage" => Some(Scope::Triage),
+        "History" => Some(Scope::History),
+        "Search" => Some(Scope::Search),
+        _ => None,
+    }
+}
+
+/// Resolve a key input to an action.
+///
+/// Resolution order:
+/// 1. Global bindings (always checked first).
+/// 2. View-specific bindings for the active view.
+/// 3. Fallback heuristics for unbound printable characters:
+///    - Panes/History: `FilterAppendChar` for non-control chars
+///    - Events: `EventsFilterDigit` for ASCII digits
+///    - Search: `FilterAppendChar` for any char
+///    - Triage: `TriageNumberedAction` for digits 1-9
+///
+/// Returns `None` if the key is not bound in the current context.
+pub fn resolve(input: &KeyInput, view_name: &str) -> Option<Action> {
+    // 1. Global bindings
+    //
+    // Special case: `r` without Ctrl is Refresh globally, but `Ctrl+R` in
+    // Search is SearchRunSaved.  The legacy code checks `!CONTROL` for `r`.
+    // Since global is checked first, we need to skip global `r` when Ctrl is
+    // pressed.
+    for b in KEYMAP.iter().filter(|b| b.scope == Scope::Global) {
+        if b.pattern.matches(input) {
+            // Skip global `r` when Ctrl is held (let view-specific handle it)
+            if matches!(b.action, Action::Refresh) && input.ctrl {
+                continue;
+            }
+            return Some(b.action);
+        }
+    }
+
+    // 2. View-specific bindings
+    if let Some(scope) = view_scope(view_name) {
+        for b in KEYMAP.iter().filter(|b| b.scope == scope) {
+            if b.pattern.matches(input) {
+                return Some(b.action);
+            }
+        }
+
+        // 3. Fallback heuristics for unbound printable chars
+        if !input.ctrl && !input.alt {
+            if let Key::Char(ch) = input.key {
+                match scope {
+                    Scope::Panes | Scope::History => {
+                        if !ch.is_control() {
+                            return Some(Action::FilterAppendChar(ch));
+                        }
+                    }
+                    Scope::Events => {
+                        if ch.is_ascii_digit() {
+                            return Some(Action::EventsFilterDigit(ch));
+                        }
+                    }
+                    Scope::Search => {
+                        return Some(Action::FilterAppendChar(ch));
+                    }
+                    Scope::Triage => {
+                        if let Some(digit) = ch.to_digit(10) {
+                            if digit >= 1 {
+                                return Some(Action::TriageNumberedAction(digit as u8));
+                            }
+                        }
+                    }
+                    Scope::Global => unreachable!(),
+                }
+            }
+        }
+    }
+
+    None
+}
+
+/// Return a human-readable description of the action (for help text).
+pub fn action_label(action: &Action) -> &'static str {
+    match action {
+        Action::Quit => "Quit",
+        Action::ShowHelp => "Show help",
+        Action::Refresh => "Refresh data",
+        Action::NextTab => "Next tab",
+        Action::PrevTab => "Previous tab",
+        Action::GoToView(_) => "Go to view",
+        Action::ListNext => "Select next item",
+        Action::ListPrev => "Select previous item",
+        Action::FilterAppendChar(_) => "Type filter character",
+        Action::FilterDeleteChar => "Delete filter character",
+        Action::FilterClear => "Clear filter",
+        Action::ToggleUnhandledOnly => "Toggle unhandled only",
+        Action::ToggleBookmarkedOnly => "Toggle bookmarked only",
+        Action::CycleAgentFilter => "Cycle agent filter",
+        Action::CycleDomainFilter => "Cycle domain filter",
+        Action::CycleRulesetProfile => "Cycle ruleset profile",
+        Action::ApplyRulesetProfile => "Apply ruleset profile",
+        Action::EventsFilterDigit(_) => "Filter by pane ID digit",
+        Action::TriagePrimaryAction => "Execute primary action",
+        Action::TriageMute => "Mute selected event",
+        Action::TriageToggleExpand => "Toggle workflow expand",
+        Action::TriageNumberedAction(_) => "Execute numbered action",
+        Action::ToggleUndoableOnly => "Toggle undoable only",
+        Action::SearchNextSaved => "Next saved search",
+        Action::SearchPrevSaved => "Previous saved search",
+        Action::SearchRunSaved => "Run saved search",
+        Action::SearchToggleSaved => "Toggle saved search",
+        Action::SearchExecute => "Execute search",
+    }
+}
+
+/// Return all bindings for a given scope (for building help text).
+pub fn bindings_for_scope(scope: Scope) -> Vec<(&'static KeyPattern, Action)> {
+    KEYMAP
+        .iter()
+        .filter(|b| b.scope == scope)
+        .map(|b| (&b.pattern, b.action))
+        .collect()
+}

+    // -- global parity --

+    }
+
+    #[test]
